title: The Small Compound Sauces
introduction: In order that the classification of the small sauces should be clear and methodical, I divide them into three parts.
The first part includes the small brown sauces; the second deals with the small white sauces and those suited to this part of the classification; while the third is concerned with the English sauces.

entries.title: SAUCE BIGARRADE
entries.yield: This sauce is principally used to accompany braised and poëled ducklings.
notes: For braised ducklings: After having strained the braising sauce, completely remove its grease, and reduce it until it is very dense. Strain it once more through muslin, twisting the latter; then, in order to bring the sauce to its normal consistence, add the juice of six oranges and one lemon per quart (946 ml) of sauce. Finish with a small piece of lemon and orange rind cut regularly and finely, Julienne-fashion, and scalded for five minutes.

For poëled ducklings: Strain the poëling stock for ducklings or wild ducks through linen; entirely remove the grease, and add four pieces of caramel sugar dissolved in one tablespoonful (15 ml) of vinegar per one-half pint (237 ml) of stock, the juice of the oranges and the lemon and the Julienne of rinds, as for the braised-ducklings sauce indicated above.

entries.title: SAUCE BORDELAISE
entries.yield: It is especially suitable for grilled butcher’s meat.
ingredients: 2 oz (57 g) very finely minced shallots
4 oz (113 g) beef-marrow, cut into slices or cubes
3 oz (85 g) butter per pint of sauce (optional)
instructions: Put the minced shallots, red wine, mignonette pepper, and bits of thyme and bay into a vegetable-pan.
Reduce the wine by three-quarters, and add one-half pint (237 ml) of half-glaze.
Keep the sauce simmering for half an hour; despumate it from time to time, and strain it through linen or a sieve.
When dishing it up, finish it with the dissolved meat glaze, a few drops of lemon-juice, and the beef-marrow, cut into slices or cubes and poached in slightly salted boiling water.
notes: This sauce may be buttered to the extent of about three oz (85 g) per pint (473 ml), which makes it smoother, but less clear.

entries.title: CHASSEUR SAUCE (Escoffier’s Method)
instructions: Peel and mince the mushrooms.
Heat the butter and olive oil in a vegetable-pan; put in the mushrooms, and fry them quickly until they are slightly browned.
Add the minced shallots, and immediately remove half the butter.
Pour the white wine and liqueur brandy into the stewpan; reduce this liquid to half.
Finish the sauce with the half-glaze, tomato sauce, and meat-glaze.
Set to boil for five minutes more, and complete with the chopped parsley.

entries.title: BROWN CHAUD-FROID SAUCE
instructions: Put the half-glaze into a sauté-pan with the truffle essence.
Put the pan on an open fire, and reduce its contents; while reducing, add the jelly to the sauce in small quantities at a time.
The degree of reduction in this sauce is a good third. After the reduction, carefully taste, and rectify the seasoning if necessary.
Mix a little Madeira or Port with the sauce, away from the fire, and strain through muslin or, preferably, through a Venetian-hair sieve.
Stir the sauce now and then while it cools, until it is sufficiently liquid, and at the same time consistent enough, to coat immersed solids evenly with a film of sauce.
notes: To be certain of its consistence, a test may be made by allowing it to cool a little.

entries.title: VARIETIES OF THE CHAUD-FROID SAUCE
entries.introduction: The following are variations on the Brown Chaud-Froid Sauce. (Recipe 34)
notes: For Ducks: Prepare the sauce as above (Recipe 34), adding to it (for the prescribed quantity) one-half pint (237 ml) of duck fumet obtained from the carcases and remains of roast duckling, and finish it, away from the fire, with the juice of four oranges and a heaped tablespoonful (15 ml) of orange rind, cut finely, Julienne-fashion, and scalded for five minutes.

For Feathered Game: Treat the Chaud-Froid sauce as indicated in Recipe 34, adding one-half pint (237 ml) of the fumet of the game constituting the dish in order to lend it that game’s characteristic taste. Observe the same precaution for the cooling.

For Fish: Proceed as in Recipe 34, but (1) substitute the Espagnole of fish for the half glaze; (2) intensify the first Espagnole with one-half pint (237 ml) of very clear fish essence; (3) use Lenten jelly instead of meat jelly.

Remarks upon the Use of Chaud-Froid Sauc: The chaud-froid sauce may be prepared beforehand, and when it is wanted it need only be gently melted without heating it too much. It ought simply to be made sufficiently liquid to give a good coating to substances immersed in it.

entries.title: DEVILLED SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce may be served with grilled fowls or pigeons. It also forms an excellent accompaniment to re-dished meat which needs a spicy sauce.
instructions: Put the sliced shallots and white wine in a vegetable pan.
Reduce the wine to two-thirds, add the half-glaze, and reduce again to two-thirds.
Season strongly with cayenne pepper, and strain through muslin.

entries.title: “ESCOFFIER” DEVILLED SAUCE
entries.introduction: This sauce, which may be bought ready-made, is admirably fitted to accompany grilled fish and grills in general. In order to make it ready, all that is needed is to add its own volume of fresh butter to it, the latter being previously well softened so as to ensure its perfect mixture with the sauce.

entries.title: GENEVOISE SAUCE
ingredients: 2 lbs (907 g) head of salmon and remains or bones of fish
A few drops of anchovy essence
4 oz (113 g) butter per quart of sauce
instructions: Heat the 2 oz (57 g) of butter in a stewpan; insert the Mirepoix. (Recipe 228)
Slightly brown, add the head of salmon and remains of fish, and stew with lid on for twenty minutes.
Let the stewpan lean slightly to one side, so that the butter may be drained; moisten with one bottle of red wine and reduce the wine by half.
Add one pint (473 ml) of Lenten Espagnole, and allow to cook gently for half an hour.
Rub the sauce through a sieve, pressing it so as to extract all the essence. Let it rest awhile; carefully remove the fat which has risen to the surface.
Add the burnt brandy, one-half pint (237 ml) of red wine, and as much fish fumet. Boil again, then move stewpan to the side of fire to despumate for one and one-half hours.
Frequently remove what the ebullition causes to rise to the surface. Strain it through muslin or tammy.
Finish at the last minute with a few drops of anchovy essence and four oz (113 g) of butter per quart (946 ml) of sauce.
notes: The Genevoise Sauce, like all red-wine sauces, may be served without being buttered. It is thus clearer and more sightly in colour, but the addition of butter in small quantities makes it mellower and more palatable.

entries.title: REMARKS ON RED-WINE SAUCES
introduction: In the general repertory of cooking we also have, in the way of red-wine sauces, the “Bourguignonne,” “Matelote,” and “Red-Wine” sauces, which are closely allied to the “Genevoise,” and only differ from it in details of procedure.

The “Bourguignonne” Sauce is composed of red-wine accompanied by aromatics, and reduced by half. In accordance with ordinary principles, it is thickened by means of three oz (85 g) of manied butter per quart (946 ml) of reduced wine. This sauce is buttered with four oz (113 g) of butter per quart (946 ml), and is especially regarded as a domestic preparation for poached, moulded, and hard-boiled eggs.

“Matelote” Sauce is made from Court-bouillon, with red wine which has been used for cooking fish. This Court-bouillon, with the mushroom parings added, is reduced by two-thirds, and is thickened with one pint (473 ml) of Lenten Espagnole per pint (473 ml) of the reduced Court-bouillon. This sauce should be reduced by a third, strained through a tammy, and finished by means of two oz (57 g) of butter and a little cayenne per pint (473 ml) of sauce.

The Red-Wine Sauce resembles the two preceding ones in so far as it contains mirepoix browned in butter and diluted with red wine. The wine is reduced by half, thickened by a pint (473 ml) of Lenten Espagnole per pint (473 ml) of the reduction, and the sauce is despumated for about twenty minutes. It is strained through a tammy, and finished, when ready, by a few drops of anchovy essence, a little cayenne, and two oz (57 g) of butter per pint (473 ml) of sauce.

entries.title: GRAND-VENEUR SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is the proper accompaniment for joints of venison.
ingredients: 0.25 pint (118 ml) cream per pint of sauce
instructions: Take one pint (473 ml) of Poivrade Sauce and boil it, adding one pint (473 ml) of game stock to keep it light. (Recipe 49)
Reduce the sauce by a good third; remove it from the fire, and add the red-currant jelly.
When the jelly is well dissolved, complete the sauce by adding the cream.

entries.title: ITALIAN SAUCE
notes: Ordinary Italian Sauce: Put into a stewpan six tablespoonfuls (90 ml) of Duxelles (Recipe 223), two oz (57 g) of very lean, cooked ham, cut very finely, brunoise-fashion, and one pint (473 ml) of half-glaze tomatée. Boil for ten minutes, and complete, at the moment of dishing up, with one teaspoonful (5 ml) of parsley, chervil, and tarragon, minced and mixed.

Lenten Italian Sauce: Same preparation, only (1) omit the Ham, and (2) substitute Lent Espagnole (combined with fish fumet made from the fish for which the sauce is intended) for half glaze with tomatoes.

entries.title: THICKENED GRAVY
entries.yield: The thickened gravy with the veal-stock base is used for choicest pieces of butcher’s meat; that with a poultry-stock base is for fillets of poultry.
ingredients: 1 pint (473 ml) poultry or veal stock
instructions: Boil one pint (473 ml) of poultry or veal stock (according to the nature of the dish the gravy is intended for).
Dilute the fecula cold with a little water or gravy.
Pour this liaison into the boiling gravy, being careful to stir briskly to thicken the sauce.

entries.title: VEAL GRAVY TOMATÉ
entries.yield: This gravy is for butcher’s meat.
instructions: Add the tomato purée and tomato juice to the veal stock.
Reduce by a fifth. Strain the gravy through linen.

entries.title: LYONNAISE SAUCE
instructions: Finely mince the onions and brown them slightly in the butter.
Moisten with the white wine and vinegar; almost entirely reduce the liquid.
Add the clear half-glaze, and set to cook slowly for half an hour.
Rub the sauce through a tammy.
notes: The onion may be left in the sauce or not, according to the preparation for which it is intended and the taste of the consumer.

entries.title: MADEIRA SAUCE
instructions: Put the half-glaze into a sauté-pan, and reduce it on a brisk fire to a stiff consistence.
When it reaches this point, take it off the fire and add the Madeira to it, which brings it back to its normal consistence.
Strain through a tammy, and keep it warm without allowing it to boil.

entries.title: MARROW SAUCE
entries.introduction: The Marrow Sauce is a variety of the Bordelaise. (Recipe 32)
ingredients: 1 quart (946 ml) Bordelaise Sauce base (minced shallots, red wine, pepper, thyme, bay, half-glaze)
6 oz (170 g) beef marrow, cut into cubes
instructions: Follow the proportions as indicated under “Sauce Bordelaise” for the necessary quantity of this sauce. (Recipe 32)
Finish it with the beef marrow, cut into cubes, poached and well drained, and the chopped, scalded parsley.
If the sauce is to accompany vegetables, finish it, away from the fire, with the butter, and then add the cubes of marrow and the parsley.

entries.title: PIGNONS SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is specially suited to joints of venison.
ingredients: 1 oz (28 g) raisins, stoned and washed
instructions: Take the necessary amount of Poivrade Sauce and let it boil. (Recipe 49)
Prepare an infusion of juniper berries, with the water and concassed berries.
Leave the stoned and washed raisins to soak in tepid water for about an hour.
Finish the sauce, when dishing up, by adding the infusion of juniper berries strained through linen, the grilled kernels, the soaked raisins, and the Madeira wine.

entries.title: PÉRIGUEUX SAUCE
entries.yield: It is used for numerous small entrées, timbales, hot pâtés, &c.
ingredients: 1 quart (946 ml) Madeira Sauce base (reduced half-glaze and veal stock)
instructions: Prepare a base similar to Madeira Sauce by reducing half-glaze with half its volume of very strong veal stock, keeping it a little denser than usual. (Recipe 44)
Finish this sauce by adding the truffle essence and chopped truffles per quart (946 ml) of sauce.

entries.title: PIQUANTE SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce generally accompanies grilled or boiled pork, and cold meat re-dished and minced which needs spicy flavouring.
ingredients: 1 tsp (5 ml) mixed chervil, parsley, and tarragon, finely chopped
instructions: Put the minced shallots, vinegar, and white wine into a vegetable pan.
Reduce the liquid by a good half, and add the half-glaze.
Set the sauce to boil, and despumate it for half an hour.
At the last moment finish it, away from the fire, with the gherkins, capers, and mixed herbs.

entries.title: ORDINARY POIVRADE SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is suitable for joints marinaded or not.
instructions: Heat 2 oz (57 g) of butter in a stewpan, and insert the raw Mirepoix. Fry the vegetables until they are well browned. (Recipe 228)
Moisten with the vinegar and Marinade; reduce to two-thirds. (Recipe 169)
Add the Espagnole Sauce and cook for three-quarters of an hour.
Ten minutes before straining the sauce, put in the crushed peppercorns. If the pepper were put in the sauce earlier, it might make it bitter.
Pass the sauce through a strainer, pressing the aromatics; add a further one-half pint (237 ml) of Marinade, and despumate for one-quarter of an hour, keeping it simmering the while.
Strain again through tammy, and finish the sauce, when ready for dishing, with 2 oz (57 g) of butter.

entries.title: POIVRADE SAUCE FOR VENISON
ingredients: 4 lbs (1.8 kg) well-broken bones and ground-game trimmings
4 oz (113 g) butter per quart of sauce (optional)
instructions: Fry, with the butter and oil, the raw Mirepoix to which are added the bones and game trimmings. (Recipe 228)
When the whole is well browned, drain the grease away, and dilute with the vinegar and white wine.
Reduce this liquid by three-quarters, then add the game stock and Espagnole Sauce. Boil, cover the saucepan, and put into a moderate oven for at least three hours.
Take out the saucepan and pour its contents into a fine sieve placed over a tureen; press the remains to expel all the sauce.
Pour the sauce into a tall, thick saucepan. Add enough game stock and Marinade, mixed in equal parts, to produce three quarts (2.8 L) in all of sauce.
Gently reduce the sauce while despumating it. As it diminishes in volume, pass it through muslin into smaller saucepans.
Stop the reduction when only a quart (946 ml) of sauce remains.
notes: This sauce, like red-wine sauces, may be served as it stands. It is brilliant, clear, and perhaps more sightly thus, but the addition of a certain quantity of butter (four oz (113 g) per quart (946 ml)) makes it perfectly mellow, and admirably completes its fragrance.

entries.title: PROVENÇALE SAUCE
entries.introduction: In reality, true Provençale is nothing but a fine fondue of tomatoes with garlic.
instructions: Peel, remove the seeds, press and concass the tomatoes.
Heat the oil in a sauté-pan until it begins to smoke a little.
Insert the tomatoes seasoned with pepper and salt; add the crushed garlic clove, a pinch of powdered sugar, and the chopped parsley.
Allow to melt gently for half an hour.

entries.title: ROBERT SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce—of a spicy flavour—is best suited to grilled and boiled pork. It may also be used for a mince of the same meat.
instructions: Finely mince the onion and put it into a stewpan with butter.
Fry the onion gently without letting it acquire any colour.
Dilute with the white wine, reduce the wine by one-third, add the half-glaze, and leave to simmer for twenty minutes.
When dishing up, finish the sauce with the meat glaze, mustard, and powdered sugar.
notes: If, when finished, the sauce has to wait, it should be kept warm in a bain-marie, as it must not boil again.

entries.title: ESCOFFIER ROBERTS SAUCE
entries.introduction: This sauce may be bought ready-made. It is used either hot or cold. It is especially suitable for pork, veal, poultry, and even fish, and is generally used hot with grills after the equivalent of its volume of excellent brown stock has been added to it. It may also be served cold to accompany cold meat.

entries.title: ROUENNAISE SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce with duckling à la Rouennaise.
ingredients: 1 pint (473 ml) Bordelaise sauce base with excellent red wine
instructions: Prepare a “Bordelaise” sauce using an excellent red wine as the diluent. (Recipe 32)
For one pint (473 ml) of sauce, pass the raw ducks’ livers through a sieve.
Add the resulting purée to the Bordelaise, and heat the latter for a few minutes in order to poach the liver.
Be careful not to heat the sauce too much nor too long, lest the liver be overcooked.

entries.title: SALMIS SAUCE
entries.introduction: The base of this sauce, which rather resembles the cullis, is unchangeable. Its diluent only changes according to the kind of birds or game to be treated.
ingredients: Shin and chopped carcase of the bird under treatment
0.5 pint (237 ml) game stock or mushroom liquor
instructions: Cut and gently brown the Mirepoix in butter. (Recipe 228)
Add the shin detached from the limbs and the chopped carcase of the bird under treatment, and moisten with the white wine.
Reduce the wine to two-thirds, add the half glaze, and boil gently for three-quarters of an hour.
Pass through a strainer, while pressing upon the carcase and the aromatics, to extract their quintessence.
Thin the cullis thus obtained by means of the game stock or mushroom liquor (if the game be Lenten).

Now despumate for about one hour, finally reduce the sauce, bring it to its proper consistency with a little mushroom liquor and truffle essence, rub it through tammy, and butter it slightly at the last moment.

entries.title: TORTUE SAUCE
instructions: Boil the veal stock, adding the sage, sweet marjoram, rosemary, basil, thyme, bay, mushroom parings, and parsley. Cover and allow to infuse for half an hour.
Two minutes before straining, add the concassed peppercorns.
After straining through fine linen, add the half-glaze and tomato sauce (away from the fire) with the sherry, truffle essence, and a good pinch of cayenne.
notes: As this sauce must be spicy, the use of cayenne suggests itself, but great caution should be observed, as there must be no excess of this condiment.

entries.title: VENISON SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce with big ground-game.
ingredients: 1 pint (473 ml) Poivrade sauce for game
instructions: Prepare a Poivrade sauce for game. (Recipe 50)
Finish this sauce with the red-currant jelly, previously dissolved, and mixed with the fresh cream per pint (473 ml) of sauce. This addition must be made away from the fire.

entries.title: AMERICAN SAUCE
entries.introduction: This sauce is that of lobster prepared “à l’Américaine”. (Recipe 939) As it generally accompanies a fish, the meat of the lobster or lobsters which have served in its preparation is sliced and used as the garnish of the fish.

entries.title: ANCHOVY SAUCE
ingredients: 1 oz (28 g) anchovy fillets, washed, sponged, and cut into small pieces
instructions: Put the unbuttered Normande Sauce into a small stewpan. (Recipe 99)
Finish it, away from the fire, with the anchovy butter and anchovy fillets.

entries.title: AURORE SAUCE
ingredients: 0.5 pint (237 ml) very red tomato purée (Recipe 29)
instructions: Into the boiling velouté, put the tomato purée and mix the two. (Recipe 29)
Let the sauce boil a little, pass it through a tammy, and finish, away from the fire, with the butter.

entries.title: LENTEN AURORE SAUCE
ingredients: 0.5 pint (237 ml) very red tomato purée (Recipe 29)
instructions: This sauce is made like the preceding one (Recipe 60), with the same quantities of velouté and tomato purée, replacing ordinary velouté by fish velouté.

entries.title: BÉARNAISE SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve it with grilled, butcher’s meat and poultry.
instructions: Put the chopped shallots, tarragon stalks, chervil, mignonette pepper, salt, and vinegar into a small stewpan.
Reduce the vinegar by two-thirds, take off the fire, and let the stewpan cool a little.
Add the yolks of five eggs to this reduction.

Put the stewpan on a low fire and gradually combine the melted butter with the yolks. Whisk the sauce briskly to ensure the cooking of the yolks, which alone, by gradual cooking, effect the leason of the sauce.
When the butter is combined with the sauce, rub the latter through a tammy.
Finish it with the chervil parings and chopped tarragon leaves. Complete the seasoning with a suspicion of cayenne.
notes: This sauce should not be served very hot, as it is really a mayonnaise with butter. It need only be tepid, for it would probably turn if it were over-heated.

entries.title: BÉARNAISE SAUCE WITH MEAT GLAZE, OTHERWISE VALOIS SAUCE OR FOYOT SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve it with butcher’s meat.
ingredients: 3 tbsp (45 ml) dissolved pale meat glaze
instructions: Prepare a Béarnaise sauce as explained in Recipe 62.
Complete it with the dissolved pale meat glaze, which may be added in small quantities at a time.

entries.title: BÉARNAISE TOMATÉE SAUCE OR CHORON SAUCE
entries.yield: This is proper to “Tournedos Choron,” but it may accompany grilled poultry and white, butcher’s meat.
ingredients: 0.33 pint (156 ml) very red tomato purée
instructions: Proceed in exactly the same way as for Béarnaise Sauce. (Recipe 62)
When the sauce is made and rubbed through tammy, finish it with the very red tomato purée.
In this case the final addition of chervil and tarragon should not be made.

entries.title: BERCY SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve with medium-sized poached fish.
ingredients: 0.5 pint (237 ml) fish fumet or fish liquor
A few drops of fish glaze
instructions: Heat the chopped shallots.
Moisten with the white wine and fish fumet (or the liquor of a fish similar to the one the sauce is to accompany).
Reduce to a good third, add the velouté, let the sauce boil some time.
Finish it, away from the fire, with the butter (added by degrees), a few drops of fish glaze, the lemon juice, and the chopped parsley.

entries.title: BUTTER SAUCE
instructions: Mix the sifted flour with the melted butter.
Dilute with the boiling, salted water. Stir briskly to ensure a perfect leason, and do not allow to boil.
Add immediately the egg yolks mixed with the cream and the lemon juice.
Rub through a tammy, and finish the sauce with the fresh butter.
notes: Be careful that the sauce does not boil after it has been thickened.

entries.title: BONNEFOY SAUCE, OR WHITE BORDELAISE SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve it with grilled fish and grilled white meat.
ingredients: 0.5 pint (237 ml) Graves, Sauterne, or any other excellent white Bordeaux
instructions: Put the minced shallots and white Bordeaux wine in a stewpan.
Reduce the wine almost entirely, add the velouté, let it simmer twenty minutes, and rub it through a tammy.
Finish it, away from the fire, with the butter and a little chopped tarragon.

entries.title: CAPER SAUCE
entries.yield: It frequently accompanies boiled fish of all kinds.
instructions: Prepare a Butter Sauce and add two tablespoonfuls (30 ml) of capers per pint (473 ml) of sauce. (Recipe 66)

entries.title: CARDINAL SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is poured over the fish.
ingredients: 3 oz (85 g) very red lobster butter (Recipe 149)
instructions: Boil the Béchamel, to which add the fish fumet and a little truffle essence, and reduce by a quarter.
Finish the sauce, when dishing up, with the cream and the very red lobster butter. (Recipe 149)

entries.title: MUSHROOM SAUCE
notes: If this be intended for poultry, add one-fifth pint (95 ml) of mushroom liquor and eight oz (227 g) of button-mushroom heads turned or channelled and cooked, to one pint (473 ml) of very stiff Allemande Sauce.
If it be intended for fish, take one pint (473 ml) of fish velouté, thickened with the yolks of four eggs, and finish it with mushroom liquor, as above.
The sauce suggested for poultry may also be used for fish, after adding the necessary quantity of fish fumet.

entries.title: CHÂTEAUBRIAND SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve with grilled fillet of beef, otherwise “Châteaubriand.”
instructions: Put the chopped shallots, thyme, bay, mushroom parings, and white wine into a stewpan.
Reduce the wine almost entirely, add the veal gravy, and reduce again until the liquid only measures one-quarter pint (118 ml).
Strain through muslin, and finish the sauce away from the fire with the butter “Maître d’Hôtel” and a little chopped tarragon. (Recipe 150)

entries.title: WHITE CHAUD-FROID SAUCE
ingredients: 0.75 pint (355 ml) melted white poultry jelly
instructions: Boil the velouté in a stewpan, and add the melted white poultry jelly.
Put the stewpan on an open fire, reduce the sauce by a third, stirring constantly the while.
Gradually add the cream. When the sauce has reached the desired degree of consistency, rub it through a tammy.
Stir it frequently while it cools, for fear of a skin forming on its surface.
When dishing up, this sauce should be cold, so that it may properly coat immersed solids and yet be liquid enough to admit of the latter being easily steeped into it.

entries.title: ORDINARY CHAUD-FROID SAUCE
ingredients: 0.75 pint (355 ml) melted white poultry jelly
instructions: Proceed exactly as for White Chaud-Froid sauce (Recipe 72), substituting Allemande Sauce for the velouté, and reducing the quantity of cream to one-quarter pint.
Observe the same precautions while cooling.

entries.title: CHAUD-FROID SAUCE, A L’AURORE
notes: Prepare a white Chaud-Froid. (Recipe 72)

The sauce may be coloured by the addition of fine red tomato purée—more or less to match the desired shade—or by an infusion of paprika, according to the use for which it is intended. Paprika is preferable when not too deep a shade is required.

entries.title: CHAUD-FROID SAUCE, AU VERT-PRÉ
entries.yield: Use this sauce for Chaud-froids of fowl, particularly that kind distinguished as “Printanier.”
ingredients: 0.75 pint (355 ml) melted white poultry jelly
instructions: Prepare an infusion: Boil the white wine, and add to it the chervil stalks, tarragon leaves, chives, and parsley leaves. Cover, allow infusion to proceed away from the fire for ten minutes, and strain through linen.
Add this infusion to the velouté of the white Chaud-Froid sauce at the same time as the jelly. (Recipe 72)
Treat the sauce as explained in Recipe 72, and finish with spinach-green. (Recipe 143)
The shade of the sauce must not be too pronounced, but must remain a pale green. The colouring principle must therefore be added with caution and in small quantities.

entries.title: LENT CHAUD-FROID SAUCE
instructions: Proceed as for white Chaud-Froid (Recipe 72), using the same quantities, and substituting fish velouté for ordinary velouté, and white fish jelly for poultry jelly.
notes: I have adopted the use of this ordinary Chaud-Froid sauce for the glazing of fillets and escalopes of fish and Shell-fish, instead of cleared Mayonnaise, formerly used, which had certain inconveniences—not the least being the oozing away of the oil under the shrinkage of the gelatine. This difficulty does not obtain in the ordinary Chaud-Froid, the definite and pronounced flavour of which is better than that of the cleared Mayonnaise.

entries.title: “ESCOFFIER” CHERRY SAUCE
entries.introduction: This sauce may be bought ready-made. Like the Roberts Sauce, it can be served hot or cold. It is an excellent adjunct to venison, and even to small ground-game. Saddle of venison with this sauce constitutes one of the greatest dainties that an epicure could desire.

entries.title: CHIVRY SAUCE
entries.yield: Chivry Sauce is admirably suited to boiled or poached poultry.
ingredients: A large pinch of chervil pluches
A large pinch of tarragon leaves
A large pinch of parsley leaves
instructions: In the boiling poultry stock, put the chervil pluches, tarragon and parsley leaves, young pimpernel, and chives. Cover up, and let infusion proceed for ten to twelve minutes.
Add the liquid (strained through linen) to the velouté.
Boil, reduce by a quarter, and complete it with the Green Butter. (Recipe 143)

entries.title: CREAM SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce with boiled fish, poultry, eggs, and various vegetables.
ingredients: 0.25 pint (118 ml) very fresh cream for finishing
instructions: Boil the Béchamel Sauce, and add one-quarter pint (118 ml) of cream to it.
Reduce on an open fire until the sauce has become very thick; then pass through tammy.
Bring to its normal degree of consistency by gradually adding, away from the fire, another one-quarter pint (118 ml) of very fresh cream and a few drops of lemon-juice.

entries.title: SHRIMP SAUCE
ingredients: 1 pint (473 ml) fish velouté or Béchamel sauce
0.25 pint (118 ml) very clear fish fumet
instructions: Boil the fish velouté or Béchamel sauce, and add to it the cream and very clear fish fumet.
Reduce to one pint, and finish the sauce, away from the fire, with the Shrimp Butter and shelled shrimps’ tails. (Recipe 145)

entries.title: CURRY SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce with fish, shell-fish, poultry, and various egg-preparations.
instructions: Slightly brown the onions, parsley roots, celery, thyme, bay, and mace in butter.
Sprinkle with the flour and curry pepper. Cook the flour for some minutes without letting it acquire any colour.
Dilute with the white stock. Boil, cook gently for three-quarters of an hour, and rub through a tammy.
Now heat the sauce, remove its grease, and keep it in the bain-marie.
notes: This sauce is sometimes flavoured with cocoa-nut milk in the proportion of one-quarter of the diluent.

entries.title: DIPLOMATE SAUCE
ingredients: 3 tbsp (45 ml) lobster meat, cut into small tubes
3 tbsp (45 ml) truffles, cut into small tubes
instructions: Take one pint (473 ml) of Normande Sauce, prepared according to Recipe 99, and finish it with the lobster butter and the lobster meat and truffles cut into small, regular tubes.

entries.title: HERB SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce with boiled or poached fish.
ingredients: 1 tbsp (15 ml) parsley, chopped and mixed
1 tbsp (15 ml) chervil, chopped and mixed
1 tbsp (15 ml) tarragon, chopped and mixed
1 tbsp (15 ml) chives, chopped and mixed
instructions: Prepare one pint of white-wine sauce. (Recipe 111)
Finish it away from the fire with the shallot butter and the chopped and mixed parsley, chervil, tarragon, and chives.

entries.title: GOOSEBERRY SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is excellent with grilled mackerel and the poached fillets of that fish.
instructions: Prepare one pint of butter sauce. (Recipe 66)
Meanwhile put the green gooseberries into a small copper saucepan containing boiling water. Boil for five minutes, then drain.
Put the gooseberries in a little stewpan with the white wine and powdered sugar.
Gently cook the gooseberries, rub them through a tammy, and add the resulting pulp to the butter sauce.

entries.title: HUNGARIAN SAUCE
entries.yield: It forms an ideal accompaniment to choice morsels of lamb and veal, eggs, poultry, and fish.
ingredients: 1 pint (473 ml) ordinary or Lenten Velouté
instructions: Gently fry the chopped onions in butter, without colouring, seasoned with table-salt and paprika.
Moisten with the white wine, add the faggot, and reduce the wine by two-thirds. Remove the herbs.
Finish with the Velouté (ordinary or Lenten, according to use) and boil moderately for five minutes.
Rub the sauce through a tammy, and complete it with the 2 oz (57 g) of butter.
notes: Remember this sauce should be of a tender, pink shade, which it must owe to the paprika alone.

entries.title: OYSTER SAUCE
ingredients: 0.25 pint (118 ml) reduced oyster liquor, strained
instructions: Take one pint of Normande Sauce, finish it as directed in that recipe (Recipe 99), and complete it with the reduced and strained oyster liquor and the poached and trimmed oysters.

entries.title: IVORY SAUCE, OR ALBUFERA SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce chiefly with poultry and poached sweet-bread.
ingredients: 4 tbsp (60 ml) dissolved, pale, meat glaze
instructions: Take the necessary quantity of Suprême Sauce, prepared as explained in Recipe 106a.
Add the dissolved, pale, meat glaze to lend the sauce that ivory-white tint which characterises it.

entries.title: JOINVILLE SAUCE
entries.introduction: As may be seen, Joinville Sauce differs from similar preparations in the final operation where crayfish and shrimp butter are combined.
ingredients: 1 oz (28 g) very black truffles cut Julienne-fashion (optional)
instructions: Prepare one pint of Normande Sauce as given in the first part of its formula. (Recipe 99)
Complete it with the shrimp butter and crayfish butter.

If this sauce is to accompany a fish à la Joinville, which includes a special garnish, it is served as it stands. If it is served with a large, boiled, ungarnished fish, the truffles cut Julienne-fashion should be added.

entries.title: MALTESE SAUCE
entries.yield: Maltese Sauce is the finest for asparagus.
instructions: To the Hollandaise Sauce, add, when dishing up, the juice of the blood oranges and the grated orange-rind. (Recipe 30)

entries.title: MARINIÈRE SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this with small poached fish and more particularly with mussels.
instructions: Take the necessary quantity of Bercy Sauce. (Recipe 65)
Add, per pint (473 ml) of sauce, the mussel liquor and a leason composed of the egg yolks.

entries.title: MORNAY SAUCE
ingredients: 0.25 pint (118 ml) fumet of fish, poultry, or vegetable
instructions: Boil the Béchamel Sauce with the fumet of the fish, poultry, or vegetable which is to constitute the dish.
Reduce by a good quarter, and add the grated Gruyère and Parmesan cheese.
Put the sauce on the fire again for a few minutes, and ensure the melting of the cheese by stirring with a small whisk.
Finish the sauce away from the fire with the butter added by degrees.

entries.title: MOUSSELINE SAUCE
instructions: To a Hollandaise Sauce, prepared as explained (Recipe 30), add, just before dishing up, the stiffly-whipped cream per pint of sauce.

entries.title: MOUSSEUSE SAUCE
entries.yield: This preparation, though classified as a sauce, is really a compound butter, which is served with boiled fish.
entries.introduction: The heat of the fish alone suffices to melt it, and its appearance is infinitely more agreeable than that of plain, melted butter.
ingredients: 2 tbsp (30 ml) very firm, whipped cream
instructions: Scald and wipe a small vegetable-pan, and put into it the stiffly-manied butter, properly softened.
Season this butter with table-salt and a few drops of lemon-juice.
Whisk it while gradually adding the cold water.
Finish with the whipped cream.

entries.title: MUSTARD SAUCE
entries.yield: It is served with certain small grilled fish, especially fresh herrings.
instructions: Take the necessary quantity of butter sauce and complete it, away from the fire, with the mustard. (Recipe 66)
notes: If the sauce has to wait, it must be kept in a bain-marie, for it should not on any account boil.

entries.title: NANTUA SAUCE
ingredients: 3 oz (85 g) very fine crayfish butter
1 tbsp (15 ml) small, shelled crayfishes’ tails
instructions: Boil the Béchamel Sauce, add one-half pint (237 ml) of cream, and reduce by a third.
Rub it through a tammy.
Finish it with a further addition of the 2 tbsp (30 ml) of cream, the crayfish butter, and the shelled crayfishes’ tails.

entries.title: NEWBURG SAUCE - FIRST METHOD (WITH RAW LOBSTERS)
ingredients: 0.33 pint (156 ml) Marsala or old Sherry
instructions: Divide the lobster into four parts. Remove its creamy parts, pound them finely with 2 oz (57 g) of butter, and put them aside.
Heat the 1.5 oz (43 g) of butter and oil in a sautépan, and insert the pieces of lobster, well seasoned with salt and cayenne.
Fry until the pieces assume a fine, red colour; entirely drain away the butter.
Add the burnt brandy and Marsala or old Sherry.
Reduce the wine by two-thirds, and wet the lobster with the cream and fish fumet.
Now add a faggot, cover the sautépan, and gently cook for twenty-five minutes.
Drain the lobster on a sieve, remove the meat and cut it into cubes.
Finish the sauce by adding the creamy portions put aside from the first. Boil so as to ensure the cooking of these latter portions.
Add the meat, cut into cubes, and verify the seasoning.
notes: The addition of the meat to the sauce is optional; instead of cutting it into cubes it may be stewed and displayed on the fish constituting the dish.

entries.title: NEWBURG SAUCE - SECOND METHOD (WITH COOKED LOBSTER)
introduction: Originally, these two sauces, like the American, were exclusively composed of, and served with, lobster. To obviate the difficulty of digesting lobster at night, I have made it a practice to serve lobster sauce with fillets or Mousselines of sole, adding the lobster as a garnish only.

By using such condiments as curry and paprika, excellent varieties of this sauce may be obtained, which are particularly suited to sole and other white Lenten fish. In either of these cases it is well to add a little rice “à l’Indienne” to the fish.
instructions: The lobster having been cooked in a Court-bouillon, shell the tail and slice it up.
Arrange these slices in a sautépan liberally buttered at the bottom; season them strongly with salt and cayenne, and heat them on both sides to effect the reddening of the skin.
Immerse, so as to cover, in a good Sherry, and almost entirely reduce same.
When dishing up, pour on to the slices a leason composed of the fresh cream and the egg yolks.
Gently stir, away from the fire, and roll the saucepan about until the leason is completed.

entries.title: NOISETTE SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this with salmon, trout, and all boiled fish in general.
instructions: Prepare a Hollandaise Sauce according to the recipe under Recipe 30.
Add the hazel-nut butter at the last moment.

entries.title: NORMANDE SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is proper to fillet of sole “à la Normande,” but it is also frequently used as the base of other small sauces.
ingredients: 4 tbsp (60 ml) good cream for finishing
instructions: Put the fish velouté, mushroom liquor, oyster liquor, sole fumet, egg yolks, lemon-juice, and 0.25 pint (118 ml) of cream in a sautépan.
Reduce by a good third on an open fire.
Season with a little cayenne, rub through a tammy.
Finish with the butter and 4 tbsp (60 ml) of good cream.

entries.title: ORIENTAL SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce in the same way as American Sauce. (Recipe 58)
instructions: Take one pint of American sauce, season with curry, and reduce to a third. (Recipe 58)
Then add, away from the fire, the cream per pint of sauce.

entries.title: POULETTE SAUCE
entries.yield: Use this sauce with certain vegetables, but more generally with sheep’s trotters.
instructions: Boil the Sauce Allemande for a few minutes, and add the mushroom liquor.
Finish, away from the fire, with the butter, lemon-juice, and chopped parsley.

entries.title: RAVIGOTE SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce accompanies boiled poultry and certain white “abats.”
instructions: Reduce the white wine with the vinegar by half.
Add the ordinary velouté, boil gently for a few minutes.
Finish with the shallot butter and the chopped chervil, tarragon, and chives.

entries.title: REGENCY SAUCE
notes: If this sauce is to garnish poultry: boil one pint (473 ml) of Allemande Sauce with six tablespoonfuls (90 ml) of mushroom essence and two tablespoonfuls (30 ml) of truffle essence. Finish with four tablespoonfuls (60 ml) of poultry glaze.
If it is to garnish fish: substitute for the Allemande Sauce some fish velouté thickened with egg-yolks and the essences of mushroom and truffle as above. Complete with some fish essence.

entries.title: SOUBISE SAUCE
ingredients: 2 lbs (907 g) finely-minced onions, scalded and dried
instructions: Stew the finely-minced, scalded, and well-dried onions in butter. This increases their flavour.
Add the thickened Béchamel; season with salt and powdered sugar.
Cook gently for half an hour, rub through a tammy.
Complete the sauce with some tablespoonfuls of cream and 2 oz (57 g) of butter.

entries.title: SOUBISE SAUCE WITH RICE
entries.yield: This sauce, being more consistent than the former, is used as a garnish just as often as a sauce.
ingredients: 2 lbs (907 g) minced onions, scalded and drained
instructions: Garnish the bottom and the sides of a tall, medium stewpan with the thin rashers of fat bacon.
Insert the onions, together with the Carolina rice, white consommé, powdered sugar, and necessary salt.
Cook gently in the front of the oven for three-quarters of an hour.
Then pound the onions and rice in a mortar, rub the resulting purée through a tammy.
Finish with cream and butter as in the preceding case. (Recipe 104)

entries.title: SOUBISE SAUCE TOMATÉE
ingredients: Very red tomato purée (1/3 volume of Soubise)
instructions: Prepare a soubise in accordance with the first formula (Recipe 104), and add to it one-third of its volume of very red tomato purée.

Remarks: 1. The Soubise is rather a cullis than a sauce; i.e., its consistence must be greater than that of a sauce.

2. The admixture of Béchamel in Soubise is preferable to that of rice, seeing that it makes it smoother. If, in certain cases, rice is used as a cohering element, in order to give the Soubise more stiffness.
3. In accordance with the uses to which it may be put, the Soubise Tomatée may be finally seasoned either with curry or paprika.

entries.title: SUPRÊME SAUCE
entries.introduction: The salient characteristics of Suprême Sauce are its perfect whiteness and consummate delicacy. It is generally prepared in small quantities only.
ingredients: 1.5 pints (710 ml) very clear poultry stock
instructions: Put the poultry stock and mushroom cooking liquor into a sautépan. Reduce to two-thirds.
Add the poultry velouté; reduce on an open fire, stirring with the spatula the while.
Combine one-half pint (237 ml) of cream with the sauce, adding it little by little.
When the sauce has reached the desired consistence, strain it through a sieve.
Add another one-quarter pint (118 ml) of cream and the best butter.
Stir with a spoon from time to time, or keep the pan well covered.

entries.title: VENETIAN SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce accompanies various fish.
ingredients: 0.25 pint (118 ml) white wine and tarragon vinegar, mixed equally
instructions: Put the chopped shallots, chervil, and the white wine/tarragon vinegar mix into a stewpan.
Reduce the liquid by two-thirds; add the white wine sauce; boil for a few minutes. (Recipe 111)
Rub through a tammy, and finish the sauce with a sufficient quantity of Herb Juice and the chopped chervil and tarragon. (Recipe 183)

entries.title: VILLEROY SAUCE
instructions: Put the Allemande Sauce, truffle essence, and ham essence into a sautépan.
Reduce on an open fire and constantly stir until the sauce is sufficiently stiff to coat immersed solids thickly.

entries.title: VILLEROY SOUBISEE SAUCE
instructions: Put the Allemande Sauce and Soubise purée into a sautépan. (Recipe 105)
Reduce as in the preceding case (Recipe 108), as the uses are the same.
According to the circumstances, a few teaspoonfuls of very black, chopped truffles may be added.

entries.title: VILLEROY TOMATÉE SAUCE
notes: Prepare the sauce as explained under Recipe 108, and add to it the third of its volume of very fine tomato purée. Reduce in the same way.

Remarks: 1. Villeroy sauce, of whatsoever kind, is solely used for the coating of preparations said to be “à la Villeroy.”
2. The Villeroy Tomatée may be finally seasoned with curry or paprika, according to the preparation for which it is intended.

entries.title: WHITE WINE SAUCE
notes: Method 1 (For glazed fish): Add one-quarter pint (118 ml) of fish fumet to one pint (473 ml) of thickened Velouté, and reduce by half. Finish the sauce, away from the fire, with four oz (113 g) of butter.

Method 2: Almost entirely reduce one-quarter pint (118 ml) of fish fumet. To this reduction add the yolks of four eggs, mixing them well in it, and follow with one lb (454 g) of butter, added by degrees, paying heed to the precautions indicated under sauce Hollandaise. (Recipe 30)

Method 3: Put the yolks of five eggs into a small stewpan and mix them with one tablespoonful (15 ml) of cold fish-stock. Put the stewpan in a bain-marie and finish the sauce with one lb (454 g) of butter, meanwhile adding from time to time, and in small quantities, six tablespoonfuls (90 ml) of excellent fish fumet. The procedure is exactly that of the Hollandaise, with fish fumet taking the place of water. (Recipe 30)

entries.title: APPLE SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce lukewarm with duck, goose, roast hare, &c.
instructions: Quarter, peel, core, and chop the apples.
Place them in a stewpan with the powdered sugar, cinnamon, and a few tablespoonfuls of water.
Cook the whole gently with lid on, and smooth the purée with a whisk when dishing up.

entries.title: BREAD SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is served with roast fowl and roast feathered game.
ingredients: 1 small onion with a clove stuck in it
instructions: Boil the milk, and add the bread-crumb, a little salt, the small onion with a clove, and the butter.
Cook gently for about a quarter of an hour.
Remove the onion, smooth the sauce with a whisk, and finish it with a few tablespoonfuls of cream.

entries.title: CELERY SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is suited to boiled or braised poultry. It is excellent, and has been adopted in French cookery.
ingredients: 1 onion with a clove stuck in it
instructions: Clean the celery stalks (only use the hearts), put them in a sautépan, wholly immerse in consommé, add the faggot and onion with a clove, and cook gently.
Drain the celery, pound it in a mortar, then rub it through a tammy and put the purée in a stewpan.
Thin the purée with an equal quantity of cream sauce and a little reduced celery liquor.
Heat it moderately, and, if it has to wait, put it in a bain-marie.

entries.title: CRANBERRY SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is mostly served with roast turkey.
instructions: Cook the cranberries with the water in a covered stewpan.
When the berries are cooked, drain them in a fine sieve through which they are strained.
To the purée thus obtained, add the necessary quantity of their cooking liquor to make a somewhat thick sauce.
Sugar should be added according to the taste of the consumer.
notes: This is to be bought ready-made, and, if this kind be used, it need only be heated with a little water.

entries.title: FENNEL SAUCE
entries.yield: This is principally used with mackerel.
instructions: Take one pint of butter sauce and finish it with the chopped fennel, scalded for a few seconds. (Recipe 66)

entries.title: EGG SAUCE WITH MELTED BUTTER
ingredients: 3 hot hard-boiled eggs, cut into large cubes
1 tsp (5 ml) chopped and scalded parsley
instructions: Dissolve the butter, and add to it the necessary salt, a little pepper, the lemon juice, the hot hard-boiled eggs, and the chopped and scalded parsley.

entries.title: SCOTCH EGG SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce usually accompanies boiled fish, especially fresh haddocks and fresh and salted cod.
ingredients: 3 hot hard-boiled eggs, cut into cubes
instructions: Make a white roux with the butter and flour.
Mix in the boiling milk, season with salt, white pepper, and nutmeg, and boil gently for ten minutes.
Then add the hot hard-boiled eggs, cut into cubes (the whites and the yolks).

entries.title: HORSE-RADISH OR ALBERT SAUCE
entries.yield: Serve this sauce with braised or roast beef—especially fillets.
ingredients: A good 0.5 pint (237 ml) of butter sauce
A good 0.5 pint (237 ml) of cream
A pinch of salt and pepper
instructions: Place the rasped horse-radish in a stewpan with the white consommé. Boil gently for twenty minutes.
Add the butter sauce, as much cream, and the bread-crumb; thicken by reducing on a brisk fire and rub through tammy. (Recipe 66)
Then thicken with the egg yolks.
Complete the seasoning with a pinch of salt and pepper, and the mustard dissolved in the vinegar.

entries.title: PARSLEY SAUCE
instructions: This is the Butter Sauce, to which is added, per pint, a heaped tablespoonful of freshly-chopped parsley. (Recipe 66)

entries.title: REFORM SAUCE
entries.yield: This sauce is for mutton cutlets when these are “à la Reform.”
ingredients: 0.5 oz (14 g) hard-boiled egg white, Julienne-cut
instructions: Put the half-glaze sauce and ordinary Poivrade sauce into a small stewpan and boil.
Complete with a garnish composed of the gherkins, hard-boiled egg white, salted tongue, truffles, and mushrooms. All these to be cut Julienne-fashion and short.
